title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: vignette explains tidycreel’s effort estimates work way . written creel biologists know surveys think carefully sampling theory. end understand three things: Primary Sampling Unit (PSU) matters variance. Horvitz–Thompson estimator turns sample daily counts population total, Rasmussen (1994) two-stage variance decomposes standard error -day within-day components. progressive count estimator differs mechanically instantaneous count estimator. concept developed first principles, illustrated worked numeric example, confirmed estimate_effort() output.

title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: instantaneous creel survey, observer selects random time given day records number anglers visible survey section. day × stratum combination treated one sampling unit. survey-sampling language units called Primary Sampling Units (PSUs). Imagine three-day survey two day-type strata: Six PSUs formed crossing 3 days × 2 strata. Six PSUs total — three per stratum. survey selected two weekday PSUs two weekend PSUs, sample contain four PSUs six. population want estimate season total NhN_h PSUs stratum hh. sample gives us observations nhn_h PSUs.

title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: stratified random sample, Horvitz–Thompson (HT) estimator population total : Ê=∑∈syiπi\hat{E} = \sum_{\s} \frac{y_i}{\pi_i} yiy_i = observed count (number anglers) PSU ii πi\pi_i = inclusion probability PSU ii (probability PSU ii selected sample) ss = set sampled PSUs sampling done without replacement PSUs stratum equally likely chosen, inclusion probability πi=nh/Nh\pi_i = n_h / N_h. Substituting HT form recognising sum shs_h covers nhn_h PSUs gives familiar stratum-total estimator.

title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: stratum hh NhN_h total days nhn_h sampled days: Êh=Nhnh∑∈shyi\hat{E}_h = \frac{N_h}{n_h} \sum_{\s_h} y_i NhN_h = total days available stratum hh season nhn_h = number days actually sampled stratum hh yiy_i = count (angler-hours angler-trips) recorded sampled day ii shs_h = set sampled days stratum hh ratio Nh/nhN_h / n_h expansion factor: one three days sampled, observed count represents three days worth effort population.

title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: Stratum: “weekday”. N=n=3N = n = 3 days sampled (available days observed). Counts: y1=150,y2=230,y3=190y_1 = 150, \; y_2 = 230, \; y_3 = 190 angler-hours. Êweekday=33×(150+230+190)=1×570=570 angler-hours\hat{E}_{\text{weekday}} = \frac{3}{3} \times (150 + 230 + 190) = 1 \times 570 = 570 \text{ angler-hours} Confirming R: estimate column (570) matches -hand result. single count per day se_within always 0 — within-day replication estimate within-day variance.
code: library(tidycreel)  calendar <- data.frame(   date = as.Date(c("2024-06-03", "2024-06-04", "2024-06-05")),   day_type = rep("weekday", 3),   open_hours = rep(10, 3) ) design <- creel_design(calendar, date = date, strata = day_type)  # n_anglers holds angler-hours (count × open hours already combined) counts <- data.frame(   date      = as.Date(c("2024-06-03", "2024-06-04", "2024-06-05")),   day_type  = rep("weekday", 3),   n_anglers = c(150L, 230L, 190L) ) design <- add_counts(design, counts) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability result <- estimate_effort(design) result$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   estimate    se se_between se_within ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1      570  69.3       69.3         0     272.     868.     3

previous_headings: Multiple Counts per Day: Rasmussen Two-Stage Variance
what: Why replicate counts within a day?
title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: two observers (observer two circuits) count section twice day — morning afternoon — replicated measurements within PSU. within-day replication something useful: separates measurement noise true day--day variation. Without multiple counts per day, tell whether variation across days caused real differences angler activity random counting error. replication can decompose total variance two components: SE2(Ê)=SEbetween2+SEwithin2\text{SE}^2(\hat{E}) = \text{SE}^2_{\text{}} + \text{SE}^2_{\text{within}}

previous_headings: Multiple Counts per Day: Rasmussen Two-Stage Variance
title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: -day component measures much estimated effort varies one sampled PSU next. computed treating within-day mean y‾\bar{y}_i (average counts day ii) PSU-level observation applying standard variance---total formula: SEbetween2=Nh2nh⋅sy‾2\text{SE}^2_{\text{}} = \frac{N_h^2}{n_h} \cdot s^2_{\bar{y}} NhN_h = total days available stratum hh nhn_h = number sampled days sy‾2s^2_{\bar{y}} = sample variance within-day means y‾\bar{y}_i plain language: variance report even single count per day, using daily mean place single count. reflects much effort differs one day next.

previous_headings: Multiple Counts per Day: Rasmussen Two-Stage Variance
title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: within-day component measures much counts vary within single day — , much morning count differs afternoon count. Rasmussen (1994) formula : SEwithin2=Nh2nh2∑=1nhsd,i2ki\text{SE}^2_{\text{within}} = \frac{N_h^2}{n_h^2} \sum_{=1}^{n_h} \frac{s^2_{d,}}{k_i} sd,i2s^2_{d,} = sample variance counts day ii across kik_i circuits kik_i = number counts (circuits) day ii Equivalently, using pooled formulation implemented tidycreel: SEwithin2=Nhkavg⋅swithin2\text{SE}^2_{\text{within}} = \frac{N_h}{k_{\text{avg}}} \cdot s^2_{\text{within}} swithin2=∑iSSd,inh(kavg−1)s^2_{\text{within}} = \frac{\sum_{} \text{SS}_{d,}}{n_h (k_{\text{avg}} - 1)} pooled within-day variance SSd,=∑j(yij−y‾)2\text{SS}_{d,} = \sum_j (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_i)^2 sum squared deviations within day ii. plain language: much counting error carrying around. large within-day component means morning afternoon counts disagree substantially — observer seeing large swings angler activity within single day, measurement error high.

previous_headings: Multiple Counts per Day: Rasmussen Two-Stage Variance
title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: Four weekdays sampled (N=n=4N = n = 4). day receives two counts (morning afternoon pm): Grand mean daily means: y‾‾=(15+25+35+45)/4=30\bar{\bar{y}} = (15 + 25 + 35 + 45)/4 = 30 -day SE (step step): Sample variance daily means: sy‾2=(15−30)2+(25−30)2+(35−30)2+(45−30)24−1=225+25+25+2253=5003≈166.67s^2_{\bar{y}} = \frac{(15-30)^2 + (25-30)^2 + (35-30)^2 + (45-30)^2}{4 - 1} = \frac{225 + 25 + 25 + 225}{3} = \frac{500}{3} \approx 166.67 -day variance (without finite-population correction, -replacement design assumed survey::svytotal()): SEbetween2=N2n⋅sy‾2=164×166.67=4×166.67=666.67\text{SE}^2_{\text{}} = \frac{N^2}{n} \cdot s^2_{\bar{y}} = \frac{16}{4} \times 166.67 = 4 \times 166.67 = 666.67 SEbetween=666.67≈25.82\text{SE}_{\text{}} = \sqrt{666.67} \approx 25.82 Within-day SE (step step): kavg=2k_{\text{avg}} = 2; SSd,=50\text{SS}_{d,} = 50: swithin2=∑SSd,⋅(kavg−1)=50+50+50+504×(2−1)=2004=50s^2_{\text{within}} = \frac{\sum \text{SS}_{d,}}{n \cdot (k_{\text{avg}} - 1)} = \frac{50 + 50 + 50 + 50}{4 \times (2 - 1)} = \frac{200}{4} = 50 SEwithin2=Nkavg⋅swithin2=42×50=100\text{SE}^2_{\text{within}} = \frac{N}{k_{\text{avg}}} \cdot s^2_{\text{within}} = \frac{4}{2} \times 50 = 100 SEwithin=100=10\text{SE}_{\text{within}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 Combined SE: SE=666.67+100=766.67≈27.70\text{SE} = \sqrt{666.67 + 100} = \sqrt{766.67} \approx 27.70 Confirming R: R output confirms: se_between ≈25.82\approx 25.82, se_within =10.00= 10.00, se ≈27.70\approx 27.70 — matching -hand calculations exactly.
code: calendar_m <- data.frame(   date       = as.Date(c("2024-06-03", "2024-06-04", "2024-06-05", "2024-06-06")),   day_type   = rep("weekday", 4),   open_hours = rep(10, 4) ) design_m <- creel_design(calendar_m, date = date, strata = day_type)  multi_counts <- data.frame(   date = as.Date(rep(     c("2024-06-03", "2024-06-04", "2024-06-05", "2024-06-06"),     each = 2   )),   day_type = rep("weekday", 8),   count_time = rep(c("am", "pm"), 4),   n_anglers = c(10L, 20L, 20L, 30L, 30L, 40L, 40L, 50L) ) design_m <- add_counts(design_m, multi_counts, count_time_col = count_time) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability result_m <- estimate_effort(design_m) result_m$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   estimate    se se_between se_within ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1      120  27.7       25.8        10     31.9     208.     4

previous_headings: Multiple Counts per Day: Rasmussen Two-Stage Variance
title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: se_within small angler activity stable throughout day. large fishing activity follows strong temporal pattern — example, active morning bite followed quiet midday busy evening. se_within / se > 0.5, within-day variation dominant source uncertainty, collecting additional circuits per day improve precision adding sampled days.

title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: progressive count distinct field method, variant instantaneous count. Instead taking snapshot random moment, observer traverses entire survey section records every angler encountered traversal. One pass section called circuit. observer moving rather counting instantaneously, estimator formula interpretation differ.

title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: Let CdC_d number anglers encountered one circuit day dd, let TdT_d total hours section open day dd. Êd=Cd×Td\hat{E}_d = C_d \times T_d CdC_d = progressive count (anglers encountered circuit day dd) TdT_d = total open hours day dd τ\tau cancel? circuit duration τ\tau hours, observer encounters anglers rate Cd/τC_d / \tau anglers per hour. full TdT_d open hours, total angler-hours : Êd=Cdτ×Td×τ=Cd×Td\hat{E}_d = \frac{C_d}{\tau} \times T_d \times \tau = C_d \times T_d circuit time τ\tau appears numerator denominator cancels exactly. result progressive count records total angler-hours directly, τ\tau needed derive expansion factor internally.

title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: Three weekdays; circuit time τ=2\tau = 2 hours; section open Td=8T_d = 8 hours day. Counts: C1=15C_1 = 15, C2=20C_2 = 20, C3=25C_3 = 25. Season total (N = n = 3): Ê=(3/3)×(120+160+200)=480\hat{E} = (3/3) \times (120 + 160 + 200) = 480 angler-hours. Confirming R (note: add_counts() stores expanded Êd\hat{E}_d values n_anglers column automatically): n_anglers column shows 120, 160, 200 — pre-expanded daily totals — season total 480 angler-hours, matching -hand calculation.
code: calendar_p <- data.frame(   date       = as.Date(c("2024-06-03", "2024-06-04", "2024-06-05")),   day_type   = rep("weekday", 3),   open_hours = rep(8, 3) ) design_p <- creel_design(calendar_p, date = date, strata = day_type)  prog_counts <- data.frame(   date        = as.Date(c("2024-06-03", "2024-06-04", "2024-06-05")),   day_type    = rep("weekday", 3),   n_anglers   = c(15L, 20L, 25L),   shift_hours = rep(8, 3) ) design_p <- add_counts(   design_p, prog_counts,   count_type = "progressive",   circuit_time = 2,   period_length_col = shift_hours ) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability  # Per-day Ê_d values stored after C × T expansion design_p$counts #>         date day_type n_anglers #> 1 2024-06-03  weekday       120 #> 2 2024-06-04  weekday       160 #> 3 2024-06-05  weekday       200  result_p <- estimate_effort(design_p) result_p$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   estimate    se se_between se_within ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1      480  69.3       69.3         0     182.     778.     3

title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: Three estimator pathways available instantaneous creel surveys: Single count per day classical creel estimator. variance -day variation; within-day component estimate. Multiple counts per day (Rasmussen two-stage) adds within-day variance component. Use design angler activity patterns within day uncertain known vary substantially. Monitor ratio se_within / se: value 0.5 means adding circuits per day improves precision adding sampled days. Progressive counts appropriate section long enough spot-check count miss part angler population. C×TC \times T formula recovers total angler-hours without needing τ\tau final estimate. Variance structure single-count instantaneous surveys.

title: Counts to Effort: Statistical Pipeline
text: Hoenig, J. M., Robson, D. S., Jones, C. M., Pollock, K. H. (1993). Scheduling counts instantaneous progressive count methods estimating sportfishing effort. North American Journal Fisheries Management, 13, 723–736. Pope, S. L., et al. (press). Progressive count methods creel surveys. Rasmussen, P. W. (1994). Two-stage variance estimation creel surveys multiple counts per sampling unit. Cited Su, Y.-S., Liu, P. (2025), equations 17–18. Su, Y.-S., Liu, P. (2025). Flexible creel survey estimators. Canadian Journal Fisheries Aquatic Sciences, 82, 1–27. API walkthrough showing functions data, see Flexible Count Estimation.

title: Flexible Count Estimation
text: Creel surveys estimate angler effort — total angler-hours angler-trips waterbody survey period. count data collected field can take two forms: Instantaneous counts (Hoenig et al. 1993): random time within survey period, observer records number anglers visible section. Effort estimated count × total open hours. Progressive counts (Pope et al., press): observer travels entire section (one “circuit”) records number anglers encountered along way. Effort estimated count × circuit travel time × section open hours / circuit travel time, .e., Ê_d = C × τ × (T_d / τ) = C × T_d τ circuit travel time T_d total open hours day d. addition, observers may complete multiple counts per day (e.g., morning afternoon circuits). multiple counts recorded per sampling unit (PSU), tidycreel aggregates automatically decomposes variance -day component within-day component via Rasmussen (1994) two-stage formula. vignette demonstrates three workflows using inline data.

title: Flexible Count Estimation
text: classic workflow records one instantaneous count per PSU (day × stratum). single count per day, se_within always zero — within-day replication estimate within-day variance. se column equals se_between.
code: library(tidycreel)  # Survey calendar: four days, two strata calendar <- data.frame(   date = as.Date(c("2024-06-01", "2024-06-02", "2024-06-03", "2024-06-04")),   day_type = c("weekday", "weekday", "weekend", "weekend"),   open_hours = rep(10, 4) )  design <- creel_design(calendar, date = date, strata = day_type)  # One count per day counts <- data.frame(   date      = as.Date(c("2024-06-01", "2024-06-02", "2024-06-03", "2024-06-04")),   day_type  = c("weekday", "weekday", "weekend", "weekend"),   n_anglers = c(15L, 23L, 45L, 52L) )  design <- add_counts(design, counts) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability result <- estimate_effort(design) #> Warning: 2 strata have fewer than 3 observations: #> • Stratum weekday: 2 observations #> • Stratum weekend: 2 observations #> ! Sparse strata produce unstable variance estimates. #> ℹ Consider combining sparse strata or collecting more data. result$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   estimate    se se_between se_within ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1      135  10.6       10.6         0     89.3     181.     4

title: Flexible Count Estimation
text: observers complete multiple circuits day, circuit sub-sample within PSU. Supply circuit identifier column via count_time_col. output tibble now contains nonzero se_within values. Total variance se^2 = se_between^2 + se_within^2 (combined quadrature). within-day angler counts vary substantially circuits, se_within can dominant variance component.
code: # Two circuits per day: morning ("am") and afternoon ("pm") calendar_m <- data.frame(   date = as.Date(c("2024-06-01", "2024-06-02", "2024-06-03", "2024-06-04")),   day_type = c("weekday", "weekday", "weekend", "weekend"),   open_hours = rep(10, 4) )  design_m <- creel_design(calendar_m, date = date, strata = day_type)  multi_counts <- data.frame(   date = as.Date(rep(c(     "2024-06-01", "2024-06-02",     "2024-06-03", "2024-06-04"   ), each = 2)),   day_type = rep(c("weekday", "weekday", "weekend", "weekend"), each = 2),   count_time = rep(c("am", "pm"), 4),   n_anglers = c(12L, 18L, 20L, 26L, 40L, 50L, 48L, 56L) )  design_m <- add_counts(design_m, multi_counts, count_time_col = count_time) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability result_m <- estimate_effort(design_m) #> Warning: 2 strata have fewer than 3 observations: #> • Stratum weekday: 2 observations #> • Stratum weekend: 2 observations #> ! Sparse strata produce unstable variance estimates. #> ℹ Consider combining sparse strata or collecting more data. result_m$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   estimate    se se_between se_within ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1      135  13.1       10.6      7.68     78.6     191.     4

title: Flexible Count Estimation
text: se_within small counts stable throughout day (e.g., lake angler density roughly constant). large activity patterns strongly bimodal (e.g., river morning bite evening bite mid-day anglers). Collecting multiple counts per day valuable within-day variation expected needs quantified rather assumed away.

title: Flexible Count Estimation
text: Progressive surveys replace spot-check count complete traversal section. observer counts every angler encountered one circuit duration τ hours. estimator : Êd=Cd×Td\hat{E}_d = C_d \times T_d C_d count day d T_d total open hours day d (period_length_col argument). circuit time τ cancels algebraically; needed internally compute expansion factor κ = T_d / τ.
code: calendar_p <- data.frame(   date       = as.Date(c("2024-06-01", "2024-06-02", "2024-06-03", "2024-06-04")),   day_type   = c("weekday", "weekday", "weekend", "weekend"),   open_hours = rep(8, 4) )  design_p <- creel_design(calendar_p, date = date, strata = day_type)  prog_counts <- data.frame(   date = as.Date(c("2024-06-01", "2024-06-02", "2024-06-03", "2024-06-04")),   day_type = c("weekday", "weekday", "weekend", "weekend"),   n_anglers = c(15L, 23L, 45L, 52L),   shift_hours = rep(8, 4) )  design_p <- add_counts(   design_p, prog_counts,   count_type = "progressive",   circuit_time = 2,   period_length_col = shift_hours ) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability  result_p <- estimate_effort(design_p) #> Warning: 2 strata have fewer than 3 observations: #> • Stratum weekday: 2 observations #> • Stratum weekend: 2 observations #> ! Sparse strata produce unstable variance estimates. #> ℹ Consider combining sparse strata or collecting more data. result_p$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   estimate    se se_between se_within ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     1080  85.0       85.0         0     714.    1446.     4

title: Flexible Count Estimation
text: Pope et al. (press) give worked example C = 234 anglers encountered 2-hour circuit (τ = 2 h) day 8 open hours (T_d = 8 h): Êd=234×8=1,872 angler-hours\hat{E}_d = 234 \times 8 = 1{,}872 \text{ angler-hours} code reproduces per-day calculation two-day survey. processed design’s $counts slot stores expanded per-day effort (Ê_d), can confirm first-day value equals 1,872 angler-hours directly. n_anglers column design_pope$counts shows 1,872 June 1st — matching Pope et al. formula exactly.
code: # Reproduce Pope et al. per-day calculation cal_pope <- data.frame(   date       = as.Date(c("2024-06-01", "2024-06-02")),   day_type   = c("weekday", "weekday"),   open_hours = c(8, 8) ) design_pope <- creel_design(cal_pope, date = date, strata = day_type)  cnt_pope <- data.frame(   date        = as.Date(c("2024-06-01", "2024-06-02")),   day_type    = c("weekday", "weekday"),   n_anglers   = c(234L, 200L),   shift_hours = c(8, 8) )  design_pope <- add_counts(   design_pope, cnt_pope,   count_type = "progressive",   circuit_time = 2,   period_length_col = shift_hours ) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability  # Per-day Ê_d values stored in the design (first day = 1,872 angler-hours) design_pope$counts #>         date day_type n_anglers #> 1 2024-06-01  weekday      1872 #> 2 2024-06-02  weekday      1600

title: Flexible Count Estimation
text: Practical guidance: collect one count per PSU, se_within = 0 se = se_between. classical creel estimator recovered exactly. collect multiple counts per PSU, monitor ratio se_within / se. large ratio (> 0.5) indicates substantial within-day variation suggests daily sampling intensity (number circuits) meaningful driver precision. progressive surveys, estimator variance structure instantaneous counts. key difference single-PSU effort Ê_d calculated.

title: Flexible Count Estimation
text: Hoenig, J. M., Robson, D. S., Jones, C. M., Pollock, K. H. (1993). Scheduling counts instantaneous progressive count methods estimating sportfishing effort. North American Journal Fisheries Management, 13, 723–736. Rasmussen, P. W. (1994). Two-stage variance estimation creel surveys. Cited Su, Y.-S., Liu, P. (2025), equations 17–18. Su, Y.-S., Liu, P. (2025). Flexible creel survey estimators. Canadian Journal Fisheries Aquatic Sciences, 82, 1–27.

title: Ice Fishing Survey Analysis
text: Ice fishing creel surveys share structure bus-route surveys, one important simplification: access points sampled certainty. Every angler leaving lake must pass single ice access point (boat ramp area designated ice-access site), site-level subsampling. means site inclusion probability always p_site = 1.0, overall inclusion probability reduces just period sampling probability: πi=p_site×p_period=1.0×p_period=p_period\pi_i = p\_site \times p\_period = 1.0 \times p\_period = p\_period , ice fishing surveys implemented degenerate bus-route design. bus-route Horvitz-Thompson estimators apply unchanged — probability structure simplified.

title: Ice Fishing Survey Analysis
text: Ice fishing surveys collect two distinct effort measures: time_on_ice — total hours angler party physically ice, including travel fishing hole, breaks, social time. easiest record commonly used. active_fishing_time — hours spent lines water, excluding travel, setup, breaks. captures actual fishing pressure precisely requires careful interviewing. effort_type argument creel_design() controls measure design tracks output column labeled estimate_effort(): total_effort_hr_on_ice "time_on_ice" total_effort_hr_active "active_fishing_time".

title: Ice Fishing Survey Analysis
text: Ice anglers fish open-air setups enclosed dark-house shelters. Catch rates effort patterns differ groups — dark-house anglers often target specific species fish longer hours. estimate_effort() function accepts argument produce separate estimates shelter type.

title: Ice Fishing Survey Analysis
text: vignette uses two built-datasets representing hypothetical Nebraska ice fishing creel survey Lake McConaughy January-February 2024. 12 sampling days weekends — common design choice since ice fishing pressure concentrated Saturday Sunday. sampling frame records survey calendar period sampling probability (p_period = 0.5 means day 50% chance sampled). interview data contains 72 interviews walleye perch catch, effort measures, shelter type (shelter_mode) angler party.
code: library(tidycreel)  data(example_ice_sampling_frame) data(example_ice_interviews)  head(example_ice_sampling_frame) #>         date day_type p_period #> 1 2024-01-06  weekend     0.50 #> 2 2024-01-07  weekend     0.50 #> 3 2024-01-13  weekend     0.50 #> 4 2024-01-14  weekend     0.50 #> 5 2024-01-20  weekend     0.55 #> 6 2024-01-21  weekend     0.55 head(example_ice_interviews) #>         date n_counted n_interviewed hours_on_ice active_fishing_hours #> 1 2024-01-06        12             5          5.0                  4.0 #> 2 2024-01-06        12             4          6.5                  5.5 #> 3 2024-01-06        12             3          4.0                  3.5 #> 4 2024-01-06         8             3          3.5                  3.0 #> 5 2024-01-06         8             3          5.0                  4.0 #> 6 2024-01-06         8             2          7.0                  6.0 #>   walleye_catch perch_catch walleye_kept perch_kept trip_status shelter_mode #> 1             2           5            1          3    complete         open #> 2             0           8            0          5    complete         open #> 3             1           3            1          2    complete         open #> 4             3           0            2          0    complete   dark_house #> 5             1           4            1          3  incomplete   dark_house #> 6             0           6            0          4    complete   dark_house

title: Ice Fishing Survey Analysis
text: Build ice fishing design using creel_design() survey_type = "ice". effort_type argument required determines column label downstream output. use p_period = 0.5 scalar (uniform period sampling probability across days). Omitting effort_type supplying unrecognized value produces informative error:
code: design <- creel_design(   example_ice_sampling_frame,   date = date,   strata = day_type,   survey_type = "ice",   effort_type = "time_on_ice",   p_period = 0.5 ) print(design) #>  #> ── Creel Survey Design ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Type: "ice" #> Date column: date #> Strata: day_type #> Calendar: 12 days (2024-01-06 to 2024-02-11) #> day_type: 1 level #> Counts: "none" #> Interviews: "none" #> Sections: "none" #>  #> ── Ice Fishing Design ── #>  #> Effort type: time_on_ice #> p_period (global): 0.5 creel_design(   example_ice_sampling_frame,   date = date,   strata = day_type,   survey_type = "ice" ) #> Error in `creel_design()`: #> ! `effort_type` is required for "ice" survey designs. #> ✖ No `effort_type` supplied. #> ℹ Valid values: "time_on_ice" and "active_fishing_time".

title: Ice Fishing Survey Analysis
text: Use add_interviews() attach survey data design. ice surveys, n_counted n_interviewed required — record many parties counted access point versus many actually interviewed visit, providing enumeration expansion factor.
code: design <- add_interviews(   design,   example_ice_interviews,   catch         = walleye_catch,   effort        = hours_on_ice,   harvest       = walleye_kept,   trip_status   = trip_status,   n_counted     = n_counted,   n_interviewed = n_interviewed ) #> ℹ No `n_anglers` provided — assuming 1 angler per interview. #> ℹ Pass `n_anglers = <column>` to use actual party sizes for angler-hour #>   normalization. #> Warning: 23 interviews have zero catch. #> ℹ Zero catch may be valid (skunked) or indicate missing data. #> ℹ Added 72 interviews: 60 complete (83%), 12 incomplete (17%)

title: Ice Fishing Survey Analysis
text: estimate_effort() dispatches bus-route Horvitz-Thompson estimator returns total angler-hours standard error confidence interval. output column labeled total_effort_hr_on_ice design built effort_type = "time_on_ice". demonstrate column-naming distinction, rebuild design using effort_type = "active_fishing_time" active_fishing_hours column interview data. output column labeled total_effort_hr_active. Active fishing hours shorter time--ice exclude travel, setup, breaks. difference two estimates reflects non-fishing portion trip.

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: vignette explains use incomplete trip interviews catch estimation roving-access creel surveys. Roving-access designs traditionally use complete trip interviews , following best practices established Pollock et al. (1994). However, incomplete trip estimates can valid certain conditions properly validated. Key principles: Default complete trips — tidycreel follows Pollock et al. (1994) best practice Validate using incomplete trips — statistical testing required Never pool complete + incomplete — scientifically invalid due different sampling probabilities Validation optional — use validate_incomplete_trips() trusting incomplete trip estimates vignette shows: Scientific rationale complete trip preference Recommended best practices incomplete trip estimation might considered Step--step validation workflow Realistic examples passing failing validation Diagnostic comparison mode research purposes

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: roving-access creel survey design combines two independent data streams (Pollock et al. 1994): Instantaneous counts — total effort (angler-hours) via periodic counts Complete trip interviews — catch per unit effort (CPUE) anglers completing trips design statistically optimal : Counts provide unbiased effort estimates (snapshot activity) Complete trip interviews provide unbiased CPUE (full trip information) Total catch = Effort × CPUE independent variance estimation

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: Complete trip interviews avoid length--stay bias. roving-access designs, longer trips higher probability sampled (’re present count occasions). incomplete trip catch rates differ systematically complete trip catch rates—example, anglers arrive early catch fish per hour late arrivals—incomplete trip estimates biased. Pollock et al. (1994) showed : Complete trips provide unbiased CPUE estimates Incomplete trips suffer length--stay bias unless catch rates stationary Pooling complete incomplete trips invalid (different sampling probabilities) Roving-access survey best practices (Pollock et al. 1994): Default complete trips Require ≥10% interviews complete trips use incomplete trips validation Never auto-pool complete incomplete trips

what: When Incomplete Trips Might Be Considered
title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: Despite theoretical concerns, incomplete trip estimates can valid : Stationary catch rates — catch per hour similar throughout trip duration Similar fishing behavior — complete incomplete anglers fish way Sufficient sample size — least 30 incomplete trip interviews Validation passes — TOST equivalence testing confirms similarity Common scenarios considering incomplete trips: Low complete trip sample size (≥10% threshold still met) Research questions specifically incomplete trips Diagnostic comparisons understand survey dynamics Exploring potential future sampling protocols Prerequisites considering incomplete trips: Sufficient complete trip baseline (≥10% interviews; Pollock et al. 1994) Adequate incomplete sample size (n ≥ 30 stable estimates) Statistical validation using validate_incomplete_trips() Understanding survey-specific fish behavior

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: tidycreel uses different estimators complete vs. incomplete trips: Ratio--Means (complete trips, default): estimator appropriate complete trips accounts correlation catch effort within trip. Variance computed using delta method. Mean--Ratios (incomplete trips): estimator treats incomplete trip’s catch rate independent observation. ’s used incomplete trips trip duration unknown (trip complete), making ratio--means inappropriate. mean--ratios higher variance can unbiased stationarity assumptions. details variance estimation, see ?estimate_catch_rate.
code: CPUE = (Total Catch) / (Total Effort) CPUE = mean(Catch_i / Effort_i)

what: Strong Warning: Never Pool Complete + Incomplete
title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: CRITICAL: Pooling complete incomplete trips scientifically invalid — regardless whether validation passes. Complete incomplete trips different sampling probabilities roving-access designs. Longer trips higher probability sampled incomplete phase, creating systematic bias pooled complete trips.

previous_headings: Strong Warning: Never Pool Complete + Incomplete
what: Pooling vs. Substitution — a critical distinction
title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: Passing TOST equivalence test mean may pool complete incomplete trips. means something different: Pooling — combining raw complete incomplete records one dataset estimating type. always invalid, differential sampling probabilities create bias equivalence test removes. TOST tells whether resulting CPUE estimates happen agree; says nothing compatibility underlying sampling mechanisms. Substitution — choosing use incomplete trip estimates instead complete trip estimates sole estimation basis. validation unlocks. TOST passes, can run use_trips = "incomplete" obtain trustworthy estimate; combining trip types, selecting one. short: validation passes → may substitute; may never pool.

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: validate_incomplete_trips() function performs Two One-Sided Tests (TOST) statistically test whether complete incomplete trip CPUE estimates equivalent within threshold. TOST explanation: Traditional t-test can reject difference, prove similarity TOST statistically proves estimates “close enough” Tests null hypothesis: |difference| ≥ threshold Equivalence concluded one-sided tests reject (p < 0.05) Standard approach bioequivalence ecological studies
code: # Validate incomplete trips using TOST validation <- validate_incomplete_trips(design,   catch = catch_total,   effort = hours_fished )  print(validation)

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: validation PASSES: validation FAILS:
code: Incomplete Trip Validation (TOST Equivalence Test)  Overall Result: PASSED  Complete trips:   CPUE = 2.45 fish/hour (SE = 0.23, n = 45) Incomplete trips: CPUE = 2.38 fish/hour (SE = 0.18, n = 120)  Equivalence threshold: ±20% of complete trip estimate (±0.49 fish/hour) Difference: 0.07 fish/hour (3% of complete estimate)  TOST p-values: p1 = 0.012, p2 = 0.008 Equivalence: YES (both p < 0.05)  Recommendation: Incomplete trip estimates are statistically equivalent to complete trip estimates within ±20% threshold. Safe to use incomplete trips for this dataset. Incomplete Trip Validation (TOST Equivalence Test)  Overall Result: FAILED  Complete trips:   CPUE = 3.10 fish/hour (SE = 0.31, n = 38) Incomplete trips: CPUE = 2.15 fish/hour (SE = 0.19, n = 95)  Equivalence threshold: ±20% of complete trip estimate (±0.62 fish/hour) Difference: 0.95 fish/hour (31% of complete estimate)  TOST p-values: p1 = 0.234, p2 = 0.891 Equivalence: NO (at least one p >= 0.05)  Recommendation: Incomplete trip estimates are NOT equivalent to complete trip estimates. Stick with complete trips only (Pollock et al. 1994).

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: validation plot shows scatter plot : Complete trip estimate x-axis Incomplete trip estimate y-axis Reference line y = x (perfect agreement) Equivalence bounds shaded region Color: blue passed, red failed

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: PASSED: - Safe use use_trips = "incomplete" dataset - Consider using use_trips = "diagnostic" compare estimates - Document validation results analysis notes - Revalidate survey protocol location changes FAILED: - Stick use_trips = "complete" (default) - use incomplete trip estimates - Investigate estimates differ (time day effects, early vs. late anglers) - Consider refining sampling protocol future surveys

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: ’s realistic scenario incomplete trip validation passes catch rates stationary throughout day.  Interpretation: Complete incomplete CPUE estimates close (within ~5%) TOST p-values < 0.05 → equivalence confirmed Validation PASSED → safe use incomplete trips dataset Plot shows estimates within equivalence bounds Next steps passing validation:
code: library(tidycreel)  # Simulate data where catch rates are similar for complete vs incomplete set.seed(42)  # Create calendar calendar <- data.frame(   date = seq.Date(as.Date("2024-06-01"), as.Date("2024-06-14"), by = "day"),   day_type = rep(c("weekday", "weekend"), length.out = 14) )  # Create counts counts <- data.frame(   date = calendar$date,   day_type = calendar$day_type,   effort_hours = round(runif(14, min = 50, max = 150)) )  # Simulate interviews with SIMILAR catch rates for both trip types # (stationary catch rate throughout day) n_complete <- 50 n_incomplete <- 120  # Base CPUE around 2.4 fish/hour for both groups (PASSING scenario) complete_interviews <- data.frame(   date = sample(calendar$date, n_complete, replace = TRUE),   hours_fished = runif(n_complete, min = 2, max = 8),   trip_status = "complete",   trip_duration = runif(n_complete, min = 2, max = 8) ) complete_interviews$catch_total <- rpois(n_complete,   lambda = complete_interviews$hours_fished * 2.4 )  incomplete_interviews <- data.frame(   date = sample(calendar$date, n_incomplete, replace = TRUE),   hours_fished = runif(n_incomplete, min = 1, max = 6),   trip_status = "incomplete" ) # For incomplete trips, trip_duration = hours_fished (time interviewed, not total trip) incomplete_interviews$trip_duration <- incomplete_interviews$hours_fished # Similar CPUE for incomplete trips (2.3-2.5 range) incomplete_interviews$catch_total <- rpois(n_incomplete,   lambda = incomplete_interviews$hours_fished * 2.35 )  interviews <- rbind(complete_interviews, incomplete_interviews)  # Create design design <- creel_design(calendar, date = date, strata = day_type) |>   add_counts(counts) |>   add_interviews(interviews,     catch = catch_total,     effort = hours_fished,     trip_status = trip_status,     trip_duration = trip_duration   ) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability #> ℹ No `n_anglers` provided — assuming 1 angler per interview. #> ℹ Pass `n_anglers = <column>` to use actual party sizes for angler-hour #>   normalization. #> ℹ Added 170 interviews: 50 complete (29%), 120 incomplete (71%)  # Run validation validation_pass <- validate_incomplete_trips(design,   catch = catch_total,   effort = hours_fished )  print(validation_pass) #>  #> ── TOST Equivalence Validation Results ───────────────────────────────────────── #> Threshold: ±20% of complete trip estimate #> ✔ Validation PASSED #>  #> Recommendation: Validation passed: Safe to use incomplete trips for CPUE #> estimation in this dataset #>  #>  #> ── Overall Test ── #>  #> Complete trips: n = 50, CPUE = 2.297 #> Incomplete trips: n = 120, CPUE = 2.312 #> Difference: -0.015 #> Equivalence bounds: [-0.459, 0.459] #> TOST p-values: p_lower = 4e-04, p_upper = 2e-04 #> ✔ Overall equivalence: PASSED # Now safe to use incomplete trips cpue_incomplete <- estimate_catch_rate(design, use_trips = "incomplete") print(cpue_incomplete)  # Or use diagnostic mode to compare cpue_diagnostic <- estimate_catch_rate(design, use_trips = "diagnostic") print(cpue_diagnostic)

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: ’s realistic scenario validation fails early-morning anglers catch fish higher rates afternoon anglers.  Interpretation: Complete trip CPUE (~3.0) substantially higher incomplete CPUE (~2.0) Difference ~33% complete estimate, exceeds ±20% threshold least one TOST p-value ≥ 0.05 → equivalence confirmed Validation FAILED → stick complete trips Plot shows estimates outside equivalence bounds (red) Correct action failing validation:
code: library(tidycreel) set.seed(123)  # Same calendar and counts as before calendar <- data.frame(   date = seq.Date(as.Date("2024-06-01"), as.Date("2024-06-14"), by = "day"),   day_type = rep(c("weekday", "weekend"), length.out = 14) )  counts <- data.frame(   date = calendar$date,   day_type = calendar$day_type,   effort_hours = round(runif(14, min = 50, max = 150)) )  # Simulate interviews with DIFFERENT catch rates (FAILING scenario) # Complete trips average full day (includes productive morning hours) # Incomplete trips are mostly afternoon interviews (lower catch rates)  n_complete <- 45 n_incomplete <- 110  # Complete trips: higher CPUE (includes morning fishing, ~3.0 fish/hour) complete_interviews <- data.frame(   date = sample(calendar$date, n_complete, replace = TRUE),   hours_fished = runif(n_complete, min = 3, max = 8),   trip_status = "complete",   trip_duration = runif(n_complete, min = 3, max = 8) ) complete_interviews$catch_total <- rpois(n_complete,   lambda = complete_interviews$hours_fished * 3.0 )  # Incomplete trips: lower CPUE (afternoon interviews, ~2.0 fish/hour) incomplete_interviews <- data.frame(   date = sample(calendar$date, n_incomplete, replace = TRUE),   hours_fished = runif(n_incomplete, min = 1, max = 5),   trip_status = "incomplete" ) # For incomplete trips, trip_duration = hours_fished (time interviewed, not total trip) incomplete_interviews$trip_duration <- incomplete_interviews$hours_fished incomplete_interviews$catch_total <- rpois(n_incomplete,   lambda = incomplete_interviews$hours_fished * 2.0 )  interviews_biased <- rbind(complete_interviews, incomplete_interviews)  # Create design design_biased <- creel_design(calendar, date = date, strata = day_type) |>   add_counts(counts) |>   add_interviews(interviews_biased,     catch = catch_total,     effort = hours_fished,     trip_status = trip_status,     trip_duration = trip_duration   ) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability #> ℹ No `n_anglers` provided — assuming 1 angler per interview. #> ℹ Pass `n_anglers = <column>` to use actual party sizes for angler-hour #>   normalization. #> ℹ Added 155 interviews: 45 complete (29%), 110 incomplete (71%)  # Run validation validation_fail <- validate_incomplete_trips(design_biased,   catch = catch_total,   effort = hours_fished )  print(validation_fail) #>  #> ── TOST Equivalence Validation Results ───────────────────────────────────────── #> Threshold: ±20% of complete trip estimate #> ✖ Validation FAILED #>  #> Recommendation: Validation failed: Use complete trips only (estimates not #> statistically equivalent) #>  #>  #> ── Overall Test ── #>  #> Complete trips: n = 45, CPUE = 3.103 #> Incomplete trips: n = 110, CPUE = 2.045 #> Difference: 1.059 #> Equivalence bounds: [-0.621, 0.621] #> TOST p-values: p_lower = 0, p_upper = 0.9996 #> ✖ Overall equivalence: FAILED # DO NOT use incomplete trips # Stick with default complete trip estimation cpue_complete <- estimate_catch_rate(design_biased) # Uses complete trips (default) print(cpue_complete)  # Investigate why estimates differ # Possible reasons: # - Time-of-day effects (morning vs afternoon catch rates) # - Trip length correlates with skill level # - Fish behavior changes throughout day (feeding windows) # - Different angler types (early vs late arrivals)

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: research purposes understand survey dynamics, use diagnostic comparison mode see complete incomplete estimates side--side without statistical testing. Example output:
code: # Compare complete vs incomplete estimates cpue_diagnostic <- estimate_catch_rate(design,   catch = catch_total,   effort = hours_fished,   use_trips = "diagnostic" )  print(cpue_diagnostic) Diagnostic Comparison: Complete vs Incomplete Trip CPUE  trip_type    estimate    se  ci_lower  ci_upper   n complete         2.45  0.23      2.00      2.90  45 incomplete       2.38  0.18      2.03      2.73 120  Difference: 0.07 fish/hour (3% of complete estimate) Ratio: 1.03 (complete / incomplete)  Interpretation: Estimates differ by <10%, suggesting similar catch rates

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: Diagnostic mode useful : Exploring survey dynamics formal validation Understanding differences trip types Research questions fishing patterns Teaching demonstrating survey concepts Sensitivity analyses Diagnostic mode replacement validation: Provides descriptive comparison, statistical test p-values equivalence assessment pass/fail recommendation Use validate_incomplete_trips() decision-making

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: diagnostic mode uses 10% threshold “substantial difference” (established Phase 17): Difference < 10%: Estimates similar, practical difference Difference ≥ 10%: Estimates differ substantially, investigate heuristic, statistical test. formal validation, use validate_incomplete_trips().

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: estimating CPUE strata (e.g., day type), validate within group: Grouped validation requires: Overall equivalence (groups combined) per-group equivalence (group separately) conservative approach prevents overlooking group-specific bias masked overall equivalence. Example grouped output: Even though overall validation passed, weekend group failed—incomplete trip estimates biased weekends. demonstrates grouped validation conservative.
code: # Validate with grouping validation_grouped <- validate_incomplete_trips(design,   catch = catch_total,   effort = hours_fished,   by = day_type )  print(validation_grouped) Incomplete Trip Validation (Grouped by day_type)  Overall Result: FAILED  Overall (ungrouped):   Complete: 2.45 fish/hour (n=45)   Incomplete: 2.38 fish/hour (n=120)   TOST: PASSED (p1=0.012, p2=0.008)  Group: weekday   Complete: 2.20 fish/hour (n=20)   Incomplete: 2.15 fish/hour (n=55)   TOST: PASSED (p1=0.031, p2=0.019)  Group: weekend   Complete: 2.80 fish/hour (n=25)   Incomplete: 2.45 fish/hour (n=65)   TOST: FAILED (p1=0.156, p2=0.234)  Recommendation: Overall equivalence passed but weekend group failed. Do not use incomplete trips. Investigate group-specific differences.

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: Two One-Sided Tests (TOST) tests null hypothesis: δ equivalence threshold (default ±20% complete estimate). Two one-sided tests: Test 1: H0: μ_complete - μ_incomplete ≤ -δ vs H1: μ_complete - μ_incomplete > -δ Test 2: H0: μ_complete - μ_incomplete ≥ δ vs H1: μ_complete - μ_incomplete < δ Equivalence conclusion: p-values < 0.05 → equivalence confirmed (PASSED) least one p ≥ 0.05 → equivalence confirmed (FAILED) mathematical details variance formulas, see ?validate_incomplete_trips.
code: H0: |μ_complete - μ_incomplete| ≥ δ H1: |μ_complete - μ_incomplete| < δ

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: default equivalence threshold ±20% complete trip estimate, appropriate ecological field data. can customize : Choosing threshold: Stricter (10-15%): High-stakes management decisions, research publications Default (20%): Standard ecological field studies, typical survey variability Permissive (25-30%): Exploratory analyses, preliminary surveys threshold balance statistical rigor realistic field variability. Consult statistician fisheries biologist specific application.
code: # Use stricter threshold (±15%) options(tidycreel.equivalence_threshold = 0.15) validation_strict <- validate_incomplete_trips(design,   catch = catch_total,   effort = hours_fished )  # Use more permissive threshold (±25%) options(tidycreel.equivalence_threshold = 0.25) validation_permissive <- validate_incomplete_trips(design,   catch = catch_total,   effort = hours_fished )

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: Incomplete trips short durations (<30 minutes) can inflate variance bias estimates. tidycreel automatically truncates short incomplete trips using Hoenig et al. (1997) recommended threshold: MOR print method shows truncation details: details truncation methodology, see ?estimate_catch_rate Phase 16 documentation.
code: # Default: truncate incomplete trips <0.5 hours (30 minutes) cpue_incomplete <- estimate_catch_rate(design,   use_trips = "incomplete",   estimator = "mor",   truncate_at = 0.5 # Default )  # Custom truncation threshold cpue_truncated <- estimate_catch_rate(design,   use_trips = "incomplete",   estimator = "mor",   truncate_at = 1.0 # More conservative: only trips ≥1 hour )  # Disable truncation (not recommended) cpue_no_truncation <- estimate_catch_rate(design,   use_trips = "incomplete",   estimator = "mor",   truncate_at = 0 # Includes all incomplete trips ) Truncation: 8 of 120 incomplete trips removed (<0.5 hours) Warning: 7% of incomplete trips truncated (>5% threshold)

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: mean--ratios estimator computes: Variance estimated using survey package individual ratios observations. survey design rebuilt truncated incomplete trips ensure correct variance computation. differs ratio--means uses delta method variance accounting catch-effort covariance.
code: CPUE_MOR = (1/n) * Σ(catch_i / effort_i)

what: Decision Tree: Should I Use Incomplete Trips?
title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: → Improve sampling protocol, extend survey hours YES → Continue → Sample size small, stick complete trips YES → Continue → Run validation proceeding YES → Continue → Stick complete trips YES → Safe use incomplete trips dataset → Stick complete trips YES → Safe use incomplete trips → Proceed Document validation results proceed incomplete trip estimation

title: Incomplete Trip Estimation
text: creel surveys: Use complete trips (default behavior) Follow Pollock et al. (1994) best practices Aim ≥10% complete trip interviews Sample size goal: n ≥ 30 complete trips per stratum considering incomplete trips: Validate using validate_incomplete_trips() FIRST Document validation results (passed/failed, p-values, threshold) Revalidate survey protocol location changes Monitor seasonal temporal changes catch patterns Never: Pool complete incomplete trips without validation Use incomplete trips without statistical validation Auto-pool trip types (package prevents design) Trust incomplete estimates failed validation

Jones, K.H. Pollock, D.S. Robson, D.L. Wade. 1997. Calculation Catch Rate Total Catch Roving Surveys Anglers. Biometrics 53:306-317. title: Interview-Based Catch Estimation
text: vignette extends effort estimation workflow covered “Getting Started tidycreel” interview-based catch harvest estimation. complete workflow involves five steps: Design: Define survey calendar stratification Counts: Attach instantaneous count observations Interviews: Attach complete trip interview data CPUE: Estimate catch per unit effort Total Catch: Combine effort CPUE estimates package uses ratio--means estimation catch per unit effort (CPUE), appropriate access point surveys complete trip interviews. estimator accounts correlation catch effort within interview.

title: Interview-Based Catch Estimation
text: start design count data workflow “Getting Started” vignette: effort estimate provides total angler-hours survey period, ’ll combine CPUE estimate total catch.
code: library(tidycreel)  # Load example calendar and counts data(example_calendar) data(example_counts)  # Create design with counts design <- creel_design(example_calendar, date = date, strata = day_type) design <- add_counts(design, example_counts) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability  # Estimate total effort effort_est <- estimate_effort(design) print(effort_est) #>  #> ── Creel Survey Estimates ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Method: Total #> Variance: Taylor linearization #> Confidence level: 95% #>  #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   estimate    se se_between se_within ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     372.  13.2       13.2         0     344.     401.    14

title: Interview-Based Catch Estimation
text: Next, attach interview data containing catch effort complete trips: add_interviews() function maps interview data columns design structure. design now shows count interview data attached. Interview data treated parallel data stream count data—two datasets need align specific dates.
code: # Load example interview data data(example_interviews) head(example_interviews) #>         date hours_fished catch_total catch_kept trip_status trip_duration #> 1 2024-06-01          2.0           5          2    complete           2.0 #> 2 2024-06-01          3.5           8          5    complete           3.5 #> 3 2024-06-02          1.5           2          1    complete           1.5 #> 4 2024-06-02          2.0           3          2  incomplete           1.0 #> 5 2024-06-03          2.5           6          3    complete           2.5 #> 6 2024-06-03          4.0          12          8    complete           4.0 #>   interview_id angler_type angler_method species_sought n_anglers refused #> 1            1        bank          bait        walleye         2   FALSE #> 2            2        boat    artificial        walleye         1   FALSE #> 3            3        bank          bait           bass         3   FALSE #> 4            4        bank           fly        panfish         2   FALSE #> 5            5        boat    artificial        walleye         1   FALSE #> 6            6        boat          bait           bass         4   FALSE  # Attach interviews to the design design <- add_interviews(design, example_interviews,   catch = catch_total,   effort = hours_fished,   harvest = catch_kept,   trip_status = trip_status,   trip_duration = trip_duration ) #> ℹ No `n_anglers` provided — assuming 1 angler per interview. #> ℹ Pass `n_anglers = <column>` to use actual party sizes for angler-hour #>   normalization. #> ℹ Added 22 interviews: 17 complete (77%), 5 incomplete (23%) print(design) #>  #> ── Creel Survey Design ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Type: "instantaneous" #> Date column: date #> Strata: day_type #> Calendar: 14 days (2024-06-01 to 2024-06-14) #> day_type: 2 levels #> Counts: 14 observations #> PSU column: date #> Count type: "instantaneous" #> Survey: <survey.design2> (constructed) #> Interviews: 22 observations #> Type: "access" #> Catch: catch_total #> Effort: hours_fished #> Harvest: catch_kept #> Trip status: 17 complete, 5 incomplete #> Survey: <survey.design2> (constructed) #> Sections: "none"

title: Interview-Based Catch Estimation
text: interview data attached, can estimate catch per unit effort: CPUE estimate uses ratio--means estimator: total catch divided total effort across interviews. estimator appropriate access point surveys accounts correlation catch effort within trip. variance computed using delta method, accounting covariance numerator denominator. details ratio--means formula variance calculation, see ?estimate_catch_rate.
code: # Estimate CPUE cpue_est <- estimate_catch_rate(design) #> ℹ Using complete trips for CPUE estimation #>   (n=17, 77.3% of 22 interviews) [default] #> Warning: Small sample size for CPUE estimation. #> ! Sample size is 17. Ratio estimates are more stable with n >= 30. #> ℹ Variance estimates may be unstable with n < 30. print(cpue_est) #>  #> ── Creel Survey Estimates ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Method: Ratio-of-Means CPUE #> Variance: Taylor linearization #> Confidence level: 95% #>  #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   estimate    se ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     2.29 0.114     2.06     2.51    17

title: Interview-Based Catch Estimation
text: can combine effort CPUE estimates compute total catch: total catch estimate multiplies effort CPUE estimates. variance computed using delta method formula: Var(E × C) = E² Var(C) + C² Var(E) formula assumes independence count interview data streams, appropriate since collected separate sampling processes. See ?estimate_total_catch details variance propagation.
code: # Estimate total catch total_catch_est <- estimate_total_catch(design) #> ℹ Using complete trips for CPUE estimation #>   (n=17, 77.3% of 22 interviews) [default] #> Warning: Small sample size for CPUE estimation. #> ! Sample size is 17. Ratio estimates are more stable with n >= 30. #> ℹ Variance estimates may be unstable with n < 30. print(total_catch_est) #>  #> ── Creel Survey Estimates ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Method: Total Catch (Effort × CPUE) #> Variance: Taylor linearization #> Confidence level: 95% #>  #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   estimate    se ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     851.  52.0     749.     953.    17

title: Interview-Based Catch Estimation
text: package distinguishes total catch (fish caught) harvest (fish kept). can estimate harvest per unit effort (HPUE) total harvest: Harvest estimation uses ratio--means approach CPUE, harvest column (catch_kept) numerator. expected, total harvest lower total catch since caught fish kept.
code: # Estimate HPUE hpue_est <- estimate_harvest_rate(design) #> Warning: Small sample size for harvest estimation. #> ! Sample size is 22. Ratio estimates are more stable with n >= 30. #> ℹ Variance estimates may be unstable with n < 30. print(hpue_est) #>  #> ── Creel Survey Estimates ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Method: Ratio-of-Means HPUE #> Variance: Taylor linearization #> Confidence level: 95% #>  #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   estimate    se ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     1.36 0.101     1.16     1.56    22  # Estimate total harvest total_harvest_est <- estimate_total_harvest(design) #> Warning: Small sample size for harvest estimation. #> ! Sample size is 22. Ratio estimates are more stable with n >= 30. #> ℹ Variance estimates may be unstable with n < 30. print(total_harvest_est) #>  #> ── Creel Survey Estimates ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Method: Total Harvest (Effort × HPUE) #> Variance: Taylor linearization #> Confidence level: 95% #>  #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   estimate    se ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     508.  41.8     426.     590.    22

title: Interview-Based Catch Estimation
text: Like effort estimation, CPUE total catch functions support grouped estimation using parameter: Note: example data insufficient interview sample sizes grouped estimation (weekend n=9, weekday n=13). Ratio estimation requires least 10 observations per group stability. real surveys, aim least 30 interviews per stratum reliable grouped estimates.
code: # Estimate CPUE by day_type (not evaluated - example data too small) cpue_by_day <- estimate_catch_rate(design, by = day_type)  # Estimate total catch by day_type total_catch_by_day <- estimate_total_catch(design, by = day_type)

title: Interview-Based Catch Estimation
text: Like effort estimation, CPUE estimation supports multiple variance methods. default Taylor linearization, bootstrap jackknife also available: three methods produce similar results data. Use bootstrap jackknife want verify Taylor linearization assumptions working complex grouped estimates. use method: Taylor linearization (default): Computationally efficient appropriate smooth statistics. recommended default. Bootstrap: Use working non-smooth statistics want verify Taylor linearization assumptions. computationally intensive. Jackknife: Alternative resampling method deterministic (unlike bootstrap). Useful verification bootstrap slow.
code: # Bootstrap variance estimation set.seed(42) # For reproducibility cpue_boot <- estimate_catch_rate(design, variance = "bootstrap") #> ℹ Using complete trips for CPUE estimation #>   (n=17, 77.3% of 22 interviews) [default] #> Warning: Small sample size for CPUE estimation. #> ! Sample size is 17. Ratio estimates are more stable with n >= 30. #> ℹ Variance estimates may be unstable with n < 30.  # Jackknife variance estimation cpue_jk <- estimate_catch_rate(design, variance = "jackknife") #> ℹ Using complete trips for CPUE estimation #>   (n=17, 77.3% of 22 interviews) [default] #> Warning: Small sample size for CPUE estimation. #> ! Sample size is 17. Ratio estimates are more stable with n >= 30. #> ℹ Variance estimates may be unstable with n < 30.

title: Interview-Based Catch Estimation
text: ’s full pipeline single workflow: demonstrates complete v0.2.0 workflow survey design total catch harvest estimation.
code: # Load data data(example_calendar) data(example_counts) data(example_interviews)  # Build design and attach data complete_design <- creel_design(example_calendar, date = date, strata = day_type) |>   add_counts(example_counts) |>   add_interviews(example_interviews,     catch = catch_total,     effort = hours_fished,     harvest = catch_kept,     trip_status = trip_status,     trip_duration = trip_duration   ) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability #> ℹ No `n_anglers` provided — assuming 1 angler per interview. #> ℹ Pass `n_anglers = <column>` to use actual party sizes for angler-hour #>   normalization. #> ℹ Added 22 interviews: 17 complete (77%), 5 incomplete (23%)  # Compute all estimates effort <- estimate_effort(complete_design) cpue <- estimate_catch_rate(complete_design) #> ℹ Using complete trips for CPUE estimation #>   (n=17, 77.3% of 22 interviews) [default] #> Warning: Small sample size for CPUE estimation. #> ! Sample size is 17. Ratio estimates are more stable with n >= 30. #> ℹ Variance estimates may be unstable with n < 30. hpue <- estimate_harvest_rate(complete_design) #> Warning: Small sample size for harvest estimation. #> ! Sample size is 22. Ratio estimates are more stable with n >= 30. #> ℹ Variance estimates may be unstable with n < 30. total_catch <- estimate_total_catch(complete_design) #> ℹ Using complete trips for CPUE estimation #>   (n=17, 77.3% of 22 interviews) [default] #> Warning: Small sample size for CPUE estimation. #> ! Sample size is 17. Ratio estimates are more stable with n >= 30. #> ℹ Variance estimates may be unstable with n < 30. total_harvest <- estimate_total_harvest(complete_design) #> Warning: Small sample size for harvest estimation. #> ! Sample size is 22. Ratio estimates are more stable with n >= 30. #> ℹ Variance estimates may be unstable with n < 30.  # Print key results print(effort) #>  #> ── Creel Survey Estimates ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Method: Total #> Variance: Taylor linearization #> Confidence level: 95% #>  #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   estimate    se se_between se_within ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     372.  13.2       13.2         0     344.     401.    14 print(total_catch) #>  #> ── Creel Survey Estimates ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Method: Total Catch (Effort × CPUE) #> Variance: Taylor linearization #> Confidence level: 95% #>  #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   estimate    se ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     851.  52.0     749.     953.    17 print(total_harvest) #>  #> ── Creel Survey Estimates ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Method: Total Harvest (Effort × HPUE) #> Variance: Taylor linearization #> Confidence level: 95% #>  #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   estimate    se ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     508.  41.8     426.     590.    22

title: Interview-Based Catch Estimation
text: vignette demonstrates default workflow using complete trips, recommended approach following Pollock et al. (1994) roving-access design principles. situations incomplete trip interviews want explore using estimation, see Incomplete Trip Estimation vignette (vignette("incomplete-trips", package = "tidycreel")). vignette covers: incomplete trip estimation scientifically valid validate incomplete trip estimates using TOST equivalence testing Step--step workflow validate_incomplete_trips() Examples passing failing validation scenarios NEVER pool complete incomplete trips Important: package defaults complete trips . Incomplete trip estimation requires explicit opt-via use_trips parameter estimate_catch_rate() used validation validate_incomplete_trips().

title: Interview-Based Catch Estimation
text: details interview-based estimation functions, see: ?add_interviews - Attach interview data design ?estimate_catch_rate - Estimate catch per unit effort ?estimate_harvest_rate - Estimate harvest per unit effort ?estimate_total_catch - Estimate total catch ?estimate_total_harvest - Estimate total harvest ?example_interviews - Example interview dataset effort estimation workflow, see “Getting Started tidycreel” vignette.

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: progressive count survey replaces random spot-check instantaneous count complete traversal access corridor. observer drives walks entire shoreline, access road, boat ramp circuit — counting every angler encountered along way. vignette demonstrates full progressive count workflow: scheduling, data collection structure, effort estimation, catch estimation.

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: estimators algebraically identical progressive count formula expanded: Êd=C×τ×(Td/τ)=C×Td\hat{E}_d = C \times \tau \times (T_d / \tau) = C \times T_d. circuit time τ\tau cancels, raw count CC total open hours TdT_d enter final calculation. Nevertheless, τ\tau must supplied add_counts() check field protocol (circuit duration) documented.

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: Progressive counts preferred : Anglers spread along linear corridor (river, canal, reservoir shoreline road) single spot-check miss substantial activity access route fully enumerable one traverse within count period Observer movement fast relative angler turnover (circuit time < 30% TdT_d) Instantaneous counts preferred : waterbody large complete traverse impractical Multiple sections counted simultaneously separate crews Observer presence along circuit might disturb anglers alert upcoming interviews

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: progressive count dataset needs: Calendar — sampled dates day_type open_hours (TdT_d) Count data — one row per sampled day raw angler count (CC) column recording TdT_d (open_hours value, observed value site closed early) circuit_time — single numeric value (hours) traversal duration τ\tau

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: survey 25-km reservoir access road 4-week season (July–July). single crew completes full circuit 2 hours (τ=2\tau = 2 h). access road open 10 hours per day.
code: library(tidycreel)  # Four-week season: 10 weekdays, 8 weekend days sampled calendar <- data.frame(   date = as.Date(c(     # Weekdays     "2024-07-01", "2024-07-02", "2024-07-03", "2024-07-04", "2024-07-05",     "2024-07-09", "2024-07-10", "2024-07-11", "2024-07-12", "2024-07-16",     # Weekends     "2024-07-06", "2024-07-07", "2024-07-13", "2024-07-14",     "2024-07-20", "2024-07-21", "2024-07-27", "2024-07-28"   )),   day_type = c(rep("weekday", 10), rep("weekend", 8)),   open_hours = 10 )  design <- creel_design(calendar, date = date, strata = day_type) design #>  #> ── Creel Survey Design ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Type: "instantaneous" #> Date column: date #> Strata: day_type #> Calendar: 18 days (2024-07-01 to 2024-07-28) #> day_type: 2 levels #> Counts: "none" #> Interviews: "none" #> Sections: "none"

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: row one circuit traversal per sampled day. shift_hours column records actual open hours day (always 10, vary site closed early due weather).
code: set.seed(7) counts <- data.frame(   date = calendar$date,   day_type = calendar$day_type,   n_anglers = c(     # Weekday counts: moderate activity     18L, 22L, 15L, 12L, 25L, 20L, 17L, 14L, 23L, 19L,     # Weekend counts: higher activity     48L, 55L, 42L, 61L, 53L, 47L, 58L, 64L   ),   shift_hours = 10 )

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: Specify count_type = "progressive", circuit time τ\tau hours, column holding TdT_d: n_anglers column now holds Êd=C×Td\hat{E}_d = C \times T_d, raw count. first day (18 anglers × 10 hours = 180 angler-hours):
code: design <- add_counts(   design, counts,   count_type = "progressive",   circuit_time = 2,   period_length_col = shift_hours ) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability  # Per-day expanded effort (C × T_d) stored in design$counts head(design$counts, 4) #>         date day_type n_anglers #> 1 2024-07-01  weekday       180 #> 2 2024-07-02  weekday       220 #> 3 2024-07-03  weekday       150 #> 4 2024-07-04  weekday       120 # Verify: C × T_d = 18 × 10 = 180 design$counts$n_anglers[1] #> [1] 180

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: estimate column stratified total angler-hours 4-week season. se column standard error total; se_within always zero progressive surveys one circuit per day (within-day replication).
code: effort <- estimate_effort(design) effort$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   estimate    se se_between se_within ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     6130  249.       249.         0    5601.    6659.    18

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: Interviews collected circuit traversal. Attach exactly survey type:
code: set.seed(7) n_int <- 120 # interviews collected across the season  int_dates <- sample(calendar$date, n_int, replace = TRUE) catch_total <- rpois(n_int, lambda = 2.1) interviews <- data.frame(   date = int_dates,   day_type = calendar$day_type[match(int_dates, calendar$date)],   trip_status = "complete",   hours_fished = round(pmax(rnorm(n_int, mean = 3.8, sd = 1.3), 0.5), 1),   catch_total = catch_total,   catch_kept = pmin(rpois(n_int, lambda = 0.7), catch_total) )  design <- add_interviews(   design, interviews,   trip_status = trip_status,   catch = catch_total,   effort = hours_fished,   harvest = catch_kept ) #> Warning: 10 interviews have zero catch. #> ℹ Zero catch may be valid (skunked) or indicate missing data.

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: estimate_total_catch() multiplies effort estimate CPUE estimate propagates uncertainty via delta method, producing season-total catch combined standard error.
code: cpue <- estimate_catch_rate(design) cpue$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   estimate     se ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1    0.606 0.0389    0.530    0.682   120  total_catch <- estimate_total_catch(design) total_catch$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   estimate    se ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1    3715.  282.    3162.    4268.   120

title: Progressive Count Surveys
code: harvest_rate <- estimate_harvest_rate(design) harvest_rate$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   estimate     se ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1    0.161 0.0186    0.125    0.198   120  total_harvest <- estimate_total_harvest(design) total_harvest$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   estimate    se ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     989.  121.     752.    1225.   120

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: Pope et al. (press) give canonical calculation: C=234C = 234 anglers encountered τ=2\tau = 2 h circuit day Td=8T_d = 8 open hours. Êd=234×8=1,872 angler-hours\hat{E}_d = 234 \times 8 = 1{,}872 \text{ angler-hours}
code: cal_pope <- data.frame(   date       = as.Date(c("2024-06-01", "2024-06-02")),   day_type   = c("weekday", "weekday"),   open_hours = c(8, 8) ) des_pope <- creel_design(cal_pope, date = date, strata = day_type)  cnt_pope <- data.frame(   date        = as.Date(c("2024-06-01", "2024-06-02")),   day_type    = c("weekday", "weekday"),   n_anglers   = c(234L, 200L),   shift_hours = c(8, 8) ) des_pope <- add_counts(   des_pope, cnt_pope,   count_type = "progressive",   circuit_time = 2,   period_length_col = shift_hours ) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability  # First-day Ê_d = 234 × 8 = 1,872 angler-hours des_pope$counts #>         date day_type n_anglers #> 1 2024-06-01  weekday      1872 #> 2 2024-06-02  weekday      1600

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: progressive surveys run two circuits per day (e.g., morning evening). creates multiple counts per PSU (day), tidycreel decomposes variance -day within-day components via Rasmussen (1994) two-stage formula. morning–evening case, use count_time_col identify circuit within day: se_within column now non-zero, reflecting variability morning evening circuits within day.
code: # Two circuits per day (morning and evening traversals) cal_2p <- data.frame(   date       = rep(as.Date(c("2024-07-01", "2024-07-02", "2024-07-06", "2024-07-07")), 1),   day_type   = c("weekday", "weekday", "weekend", "weekend"),   open_hours = 12 ) des_2p <- creel_design(cal_2p, date = date, strata = day_type)  cnt_2p <- data.frame(   date = rep(as.Date(c(     "2024-07-01", "2024-07-02",     "2024-07-06", "2024-07-07"   )), each = 2),   day_type = rep(c("weekday", "weekday", "weekend", "weekend"), each = 2),   count_time = rep(c("am", "pm"), 4),   n_anglers = c(22L, 18L, 20L, 24L, 55L, 48L, 62L, 58L),   shift_hours = 12 )  # Note: count_time_col for within-day identification des_2p <- add_counts(des_2p, cnt_2p, count_time_col = count_time) #> Warning in svydesign.default(ids = psu_formula, strata = strata_formula, : No #> weights or probabilities supplied, assuming equal probability  est_2p <- estimate_effort(des_2p) #> Warning: 2 strata have fewer than 3 observations: #> • Stratum weekday: 2 observations #> • Stratum weekend: 2 observations #> ! Sparse strata produce unstable variance estimates. #> ℹ Consider combining sparse strata or collecting more data. est_2p$estimates #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   estimate    se se_between se_within ci_lower ci_upper     n #>      <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <int> #> 1     154.  10.0       8.73      4.92     110.     197.     4

title: Progressive Count Surveys
code: summary_tbl <- season_summary(list(   effort  = effort,   catch   = total_catch,   harvest = total_harvest ))  summary_tbl$table #> # A tibble: 1 × 17 #>   effort_estimate effort_se effort_se_between effort_se_within effort_ci_lower #>             <dbl>     <dbl>             <dbl>            <dbl>           <dbl> #> 1            6130      249.              249.                0           5601. #> # ℹ 12 more variables: effort_ci_upper <dbl>, effort_n <int>, #> #   catch_estimate <dbl>, catch_se <dbl>, catch_ci_lower <dbl>, #> #   catch_ci_upper <dbl>, catch_n <int>, harvest_estimate <dbl>, #> #   harvest_se <dbl>, harvest_ci_lower <dbl>, harvest_ci_upper <dbl>, #> #   harvest_n <int>

title: Progressive Count Surveys
text: Hoenig, J. M., Robson, D. S., Jones, C. M., Pollock, K. H. (1993). Scheduling counts instantaneous progressive count methods estimating sportfishing effort. North American Journal Fisheries Management, 13, 723–736. Pope, K. L., Wilde, G. R., Gabelhouse, D. W. Jr. (press). Creel Surveys. Chapter 17 Fisheries Techniques, 4th ed. American Fisheries Society, Bethesda, MD. Rasmussen, P. W. (1994). Two-stage variance estimation creel surveys. Su, Y.-S., Liu, P. (2025). Flexible creel survey estimators. Canadian Journal Fisheries Aquatic Sciences, 82, 1–27.

what: 1 Building a design for the examples
title: Variance Methods: Taylor, Bootstrap, and Jackknife
text: three methods work creel_design object. use bundled example_counts example_interviews datasets, 10 sampled weekdays 4 sampled weekends.
code: data("example_counts") data("example_interviews")  cal <- unique(example_counts[, c("date", "day_type")]) design <- creel_design(cal, date = date, strata = day_type) design <- add_counts(design, example_counts) design <- add_interviews(   design, example_interviews,   catch = catch_total,   effort = hours_fished,   trip_status = trip_status ) #> ℹ No `n_anglers` provided — assuming 1 angler per interview. #> ℹ Pass `n_anglers = <column>` to use actual party sizes for angler-hour #>   normalization. #> ℹ Added 22 interviews: 17 complete (77%), 5 incomplete (23%)

title: Variance Methods: Taylor, Bootstrap, and Jackknife
text: Pass variance = "..." estimator. point estimate identical across methods; SE CI change. 10–14 PSUs per stratum three methods produce similar standard errors. convergence expected: bootstrap jackknife asymptotically equivalent Taylor linearization simple probability samples.
code: eff_taylor <- estimate_effort(design, variance = "taylor") eff_bootstrap <- estimate_effort(design, variance = "bootstrap") eff_jackknife <- estimate_effort(design, variance = "jackknife")  # Summarise all three for comparison rbind(   as.data.frame(summary(eff_taylor)),   as.data.frame(summary(eff_bootstrap)),   as.data.frame(summary(eff_jackknife)) ) #>   Estimate    SE CI Lower CI Upper  N #> 1    372.5 13.18    343.8    401.2 14 #> 2    372.5 13.20    343.7    401.3 14 #> 3    372.5 13.18    343.8    401.2 14

title: Variance Methods: Taylor, Bootstrap, and Jackknife
text: Use : stratum-level variance dominated -day variability sampling design straightforward stratified random sample days. Advantage: fastest; closed-form analytic variance formula; randomness. Limitation: relies large-sample approximations; can less reliable highly skewed count distributions small strata.

title: Variance Methods: Taylor, Bootstrap, and Jackknife
text: Use : count distributions highly skewed, design complex, want variance robust distributional assumptions. Advantage: makes distributional assumption; captures nonlinear variance propagation. Limitation: stochastic (set seed script reproducibility); slower (500 replicates default); requires ≥ 2 PSUs per stratum.

title: Variance Methods: Taylor, Bootstrap, and Jackknife
text: Use : prefer deterministic, delete-one variance estimate slightly conservative Taylor small samples. Advantage: deterministic; often stable bootstrap small PSU counts. Limitation: also requires ≥ 2 PSUs per stratum; appropriate designs unequal inclusion probabilities unless correct jackknife weights applied.

title: Variance Methods: Taylor, Bootstrap, and Jackknife
text: three methods work argument.
code: eff_grp_taylor <- estimate_effort(design, by = day_type, variance = "taylor") eff_grp_bootstrap <- estimate_effort(design, by = day_type, variance = "bootstrap")  rbind(   cbind(method = "taylor", as.data.frame(summary(eff_grp_taylor))),   cbind(method = "bootstrap", as.data.frame(summary(eff_grp_bootstrap))) ) #>      method day_type Estimate     SE CI Lower CI Upper  N #> 1    taylor  weekday    170.6  9.674    149.5    191.7 10 #> 2    taylor  weekend    201.9  8.946    182.4    221.4  4 #> 3 bootstrap  weekday    170.6 10.230    148.3    192.9 10 #> 4 bootstrap  weekend    201.9  8.604    183.2    220.6  4

title: Variance Methods: Taylor, Bootstrap, and Jackknife
text: three variance methods require least 2 PSUs (sampled days) per stratum. stratum 1 sampled day, tidycreel raises structured error naming stratum: error message names problematic stratum ("weekday") suggests either increasing sampling rate switching variance = "taylor" can still produce point estimate via lonely-PSU adjustment.
